item.title: “What is the cause, good sirs, what is the condition for the arising of unarisen greed, and for the growth and increase of arisen greed?”…
item.description:
“What is the cause, good sirs, what is the condition for the arising of unarisen greed, and for the growth and increase of arisen greed?” - “The sign of the beautiful.” For one who attends unwisely to the sign of the beautiful, unarisen greed arises and arisen greed increases and grows. This, good sirs, is the cause, this is the condition for the arising of unarisen greed and for the growth and increase of arisen greed.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 3.68
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Sāriputta, while a noble disciple attains and dwells in the rapture born of seclusion, during that time five things do not occur for him: pain…
item.description:
“Sāriputta, while a noble disciple attains and dwells in the rapture born of seclusion, during that time five things do not occur for him: pain and grief connected with sensuality do not occur; pleasure and joy connected with sensuality do not occur; pain and grief connected with the unwholesome do not occur; pleasure and joy connected with the unwholesome do not occur; pain and grief connected with the wholesome do not occur. Sāriputta, while a noble disciple attains and dwells in the rapture born of seclusion, during that time these five things do not occur for him.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 5.176
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, whatever is not yours, abandon it. Abandoning it will lead to your happiness and well-being. And what, monks, is not yours? The eye, monks,…
item.description:
“Monks, whatever is not yours, abandon it. Abandoning it will lead to your happiness and well-being. And what, monks, is not yours? The eye, monks, is not yours — abandon it. Abandoning it will lead to your happiness and well-being. Forms are not yours — abandon them. Eye-consciousness is not yours — abandon it. Eye-contact is not yours — abandon it. Whatever feeling arises dependent on eye-contact — pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant — that feeling is not yours. Abandon it. The ear … The nose … The tongue … The body … The mind is not yours — abandon it. Phenomena are not yours — abandon them. Mind-consciousness is not yours — abandon it. Mind-contact is not yours — abandon it. Whatever feeling arises dependent on mind-contact — pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant — that feeling is not yours. Abandon it. Abandoning it will lead to your happiness and well-being.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.101
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “What are the five mental shackles not yet cut off? Monks, here a monk is not free from lust, desire, affection, thirst, fever, craving for…
item.description:
“What are the five mental shackles not yet cut off? Monks, here a monk is not free from lust, desire, affection, thirst, fever, craving for sensual pleasures. Monks, for a monk who is not free from lust … craving for sensual pleasures, his mind does not incline to exertion, commitment, persistence, striving. If one’s mind does not incline to exertion, commitment, persistence, striving, this is the first mental shackle not yet cut off.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 16 – Cetokhilasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Here, venerable sir, many people in Sāvatthi are mostly intoxicated with sensual pleasures, living as if drunk, attached, lustful, infatuated, bound, and bewitched by sensual…
item.description:
“Here, venerable sir, many people in Sāvatthi are mostly intoxicated with sensual pleasures, living as if drunk, attached, lustful, infatuated, bound, and bewitched by sensual pleasures.” The Blessed One, understanding the meaning, on that occasion uttered this inspired utterance:

Beings are attached
To various sensual pleasures,
They see no fault
In the defilements that fetter.

Indeed, beings bound by fetters,
Do not cross over
This great, broad flood.

From: Udana 7.3
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: When feeling a pleasant feeling, he understands: ‘It is impermanent’; he understands: ‘It is not to be clung to’; he understands: ‘It is not to…
item.description:
When feeling a pleasant feeling, he understands: ‘It is impermanent’; he understands: ‘It is not to be clung to’; he understands: ‘It is not to be delighted in.’ When feeling a painful feeling, he understands: ‘It is impermanent’; he understands: ‘It is not to be clung to’; he understands: ‘It is not to be delighted in.’ When feeling a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he understands: ‘It is impermanent’; he understands: ‘It is not to be clung to’; he understands: ‘It is not to be delighted in.’ If he feels a pleasant feeling, he feels it as one who is not bound. If he feels a painful feeling … If he feels a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he feels it as one who is not bound. When he feels a feeling ending with the body, he understands: ‘I feel a feeling ending with the body.’ When he feels a feeling ending with life, he understands: ‘I feel a feeling ending with life.’ He understands: ‘With the breakup of the body, after death, everything that is felt, in which he no longer delights, will become cool right here.’ Just as, Assaji, an oil lamp burns in dependence on oil and a wick. When the oil and wick of that lamp are exhausted, with no further fuel, the lamp is extinguished. So too, Assaji, a monk, when feeling a feeling ending with the body, understands … when feeling a feeling ending with life, understands … after death, everything that is felt, in which he no longer delights, becomes cool.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.88
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Furthermore, Cunda, here a person is infatuated, addicted, living surrounded by the five strands of sensual pleasure; that is the fourth kind of pleasure. Cunda,…
item.description:
“Furthermore, Cunda, here a person is infatuated, addicted, living surrounded by the five strands of sensual pleasure; that is the fourth kind of pleasure. Cunda, these four kinds of pleasure are low, vulgar, worldly, ignoble, unbeneficial, not leading to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, and Nibbāna. Cunda, this is possible: wanderers of other sects might say: ‘The Sakyan ascetics are living addicted to these four kinds of pleasure.’ They should be answered: ‘That is not so; you speak wrongly. You misrepresent us, untruthfully, falsely.’”

From: Dīgha Nikāya 29 – Pāsādikasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: - “Venerable Sāriputta, what is happiness? What is suffering?” - “Friend, rebirth is suffering; non-rebirth is happiness. When there is rebirth, friend, this suffering is…
item.description:
- “Venerable Sāriputta, what is happiness? What is suffering?”
- “Friend, rebirth is suffering; non-rebirth is happiness. When there is rebirth, friend, this suffering is to be expected: cold, heat, hunger, thirst, defecation, urination, contact with fire, contact with a stick, contact with a sword. Even relatives and friends, when they meet and gather together, cause distress. Friend, rebirth is suffering; when there is rebirth, this suffering is to be expected. When there is no rebirth, friend, this happiness is to be expected: no cold, no heat, no hunger, no thirst, no defecation, no urination, no contact with fire, no contact with a stick, no contact with a sword. Even relatives and friends, when they meet and gather together, do not cause distress. Friend, when there is no rebirth, this happiness is to be expected.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 10.65
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Rāhula, develop the meditation on foulness. Rāhula, by developing the meditation on foulness, what is called lust is abandoned. Rāhula, develop the meditation on impermanence.…
item.description:
“Rāhula, develop the meditation on foulness. Rāhula, by developing the meditation on foulness, what is called lust is abandoned. Rāhula, develop the meditation on impermanence. Rāhula, by developing the meditation on impermanence, what is called the conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 62 – Mahārāhulovādasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, just as a forest deer, walking in the forest, walks confidently, stands confidently, sits confidently, lies down confidently. Why is that? Because it has…
item.description:
“Monks, just as a forest deer, walking in the forest, walks confidently, stands confidently, sits confidently, lies down confidently. Why is that? Because it has escaped the hunter’s reach. So too, monks, a monk, secluding himself from sensual desires, secluding himself from unwholesome states, enters and dwells in the first jhāna, which is accompanied by initial and sustained thought, with rapture and pleasure born of seclusion. Monks, that monk is called one who has blinded Māra, who has destroyed his track, who has escaped the sight of Māra.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 26 – Ariyapariyesanāsutta
📖 Read this sutta
